prompt:
Create a CampusRec Seat & Commerce Operations platform that unifies on-site program registration, seat utilization, and a compliant offline order lifecycle for a recreation center, clinic-adjacent wellness facility, or training studio operating on a private network. Regular Members browse sessions and products in an English UI rendered with Templ, using a responsive catalog with search, availability indicators, and clear registration states such as Registered, Pending Approval, Waitlisted, Canceled, or Rejected. Members can either add items to a cart or use Buy Now to check out, selecting an on-file delivery address for shippable goods and choosing an offline WeChat Pay option that generates a scannable payment request and shows a 15-minute countdown; if payment is not confirmed in time, the order is auto-closed and the UI explains next steps. For session attendance, members perform Arrival Check-In with a configurable method: staff-confirmed QR scan on a facility kiosk by default, with optional local Bluetooth beacon confirmation when available; once seated, temporary leave rules are enforced (for example, one 10-minute break per hour, after which the seat is released). No-show is auto-canceled after 10 minutes past start time, and prolonged unverified occupancy triggers an exception ticket visible to Staff. Staff users manage shipping orders, capture proof-of-delivery (signature image or typed acknowledgment), and record delivery exceptions. Moderators review user-generated posts and reports in a manual console, applying posting frequency limits (e.g., 5 posts per hour) and enforcing account bans for repeated abuse. Administrators operate KPI dashboards for fill rate, member growth/churn, engagement, and coach productivity, with filtered exports for weekly operations reviews.

The backend uses Gin to expose decoupled REST-style APIs consumed by the Templ-rendered frontend, enforcing role-based access control for Members, Staff, Moderators, and Administrators with local username/password authentication and a 12-character minimum password plus lockout after 5 failed attempts for 15 minutes. PostgreSQL persists orders, payments, invoices/e-vouchers, registrations, seat inventory, check-in events, logistics artifacts, moderation decisions, and exception/claims tickets. The seat-control engine must perform atomic seat deduction with database transactions to prevent overselling, automatically promote the next user from the waitlist within 30 seconds when a seat is released, and enforce deadlines such as registration closure 2 hours before start unless overridden by an admin policy. The payment flow must support WeChat Pay callback signature verification using locally stored merchant keys, record immutable audit logs for status transitions, and reconcile refunds by syncing local refund status to the order record; no external payment gateways or network calls are required for system operation beyond the internal network. Exception handling uses standardized types, responsibility assignment, and a ticket workflow from intake through closure, with configurable SLAs (e.g., initial response within 4 business hours, resolution within 3 calendar days) and local scheduled jobs that generate in-app reminders and escalation flags. Import/export runs fully offline via Excel/CSV parsing with strict validation, duplicate detection, and file fingerprinting; queues support async tasks like report generation and file processing. The platform includes a configuration center with canary release by user cohort percentage (e.g., 10% of staff accounts), nightly encrypted backups to local storage, point-in-time restore, and data archiving rules such as moving closed orders and tickets older than 24 months into an archive schema while keeping masked lookup fields for reporting.
